feed.title: Lundi Ngantweni
feed.subtitle: Cloud engineer, Linux systems administrator, and AWS specialist. Writing about cloud infrastructure, automation, and open-source tools.

entry.title: Preparing Proxmox Template
entry.content:
A template is a fully pre-configured operating system image that can used to deploy KVM virtual machines. Creating a special template is usually preferred over cloning an existing VM. Deploying virtual machines from templates is blazing fast, very comfortable and if you use linked clones you can optimize your storage by using base images and thin-provisioning.

  1. 1. Perfrom an system update
  2. 2. Create passwordless sudo
  3. 3. Install cloud-init
  4. 4. Confirm and clear machine-id
  5. 5. Confirm if the machine id symbolic link exists, if not you can create one
  6. 6. Remove ssh_host keys
  7. 7. Us the below text script to create one shot service to regenerate hostkeys (copy/paste below to regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service)
    1. Follow these steps to create systemd files
  8. 8. Perform a repo clean and remove stale applications
  9. 9. Clear the history file

Creating a template will automatically convert your current VM to a template, to ensure that every VM launched from the template is unique there are configurations which can be made to ensure that VMs launched from template are consistent and unique

1. Perfrom an system update

This is to ensure that your newly launched VM is launched with latest packages, this step is also generally required when launching and accessing new VM to ensure that your system is on latest available packages.

# file: "system-update.sh"
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

2. Create passwordless sudo

Passwordless sudo just makes managing VM tasks much easier, you have the option of running commands as root but this creates files and folders as root. Leaning towards security best practices using sudo just makes system more secure and passwordless sudo makes running priviledged commands much easier as it won’t prompt for password.

# file: "visudo-config"
sudo vi visudo

# Add the following entry (replace with username)
sysadmin ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL

OR

#Add the rule for all members of wheel group
%wheel ALL = (ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL

3. Install cloud-init

Install needed utilities, in this command we installing cloud-init, a tool that automates the initialization of cloud and VM instances. You can configure cloud-init to perform a variety of tasks during boot, will be covered in later chapters.

sudo apt install cloud-init

4. Confirm and clear machine-id

The machine ID is usually generated from a random source during system installation or first boot and stays constant for all subsequent boots. Optionally, for stateless systems, it is generated during runtime during early boot if necessary.

sudo cat /etc/machine-id
sudo truncate -s 0 /etc/machine-id

5. Confirm if the machine id symbolic link exists, if not you can create one

The /var/lib/dbus/machine-id is generally the directory used by dbus to store the UUID for the machine-ID, by creating the symbolic link we are ensuring that there is consistency between the two locations. This symbolic link should be there by default and creating it is optional.

sudo ls -l /var/lib/dbus/machine-id
sudo ln -s /etc/machine-id /var/lib/dbus/machine-id

6. Remove ssh_host keys

Technically, manually removal of the ssh_host keys are not needed as that is done by the script in step.7 as well, prior to obtaining the script I manually removed the keys with the hopes of having them created by cloud-init that does not always go well….

sudo cd /etc/ssh
sudo rm ssh_host_*

7. Us the below text script to create one shot service to regenerate hostkeys (copy/paste below to regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service)

Credit for this script is Jay from learnLinux TV and can be found here.

# file: "regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service"
[Unit]
Description=Regenerate SSH host keys
Before=ssh.service
ConditionFileIsExecutable=/usr/bin/ssh-keygen

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStartPre=-/bin/dd if=/dev/hwrng of=/dev/urandom count=1 bs=4096
ExecStartPre=-/bin/sh -c "/bin/rm -f -v /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key*"
ExecStart=/usr/bin/ssh-keygen -A -v
ExecStartPost=/bin/systemctl disable regenerate_ssh_host_keys

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Follow these steps to create systemd files

The below steps covers general guidance on creating a systemd unit file.

sudo vi regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service
sudo chown root:root regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service
sudo mv regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service /etc/systemd/system
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable regenerate_ssh_host_keys.service

8. Perform a repo clean and remove stale applications

sudo apt clean
sudo apt autoremove

9. Clear the history file

history -c && history -w
entry.summary: This post covers a basic walkthrough on steps I generally take to prepare proxmox VM for template

entry.title: Create logical Volume
entry.content:
In this blog, I will be covering the creation of an LVM volume. To be honest I drafted this article as I configuring my latest ubuntu server and the server’s purpose was to function as a NAS and general purpose file server. Now I know there is alot of NAS services out there with all sorts of goodies however the intention of this is to master the underlying services and overtime I can build my own management interface abd add new feather sets.

  1. 1. Check attributes of the current volume group
  2. 2. We are now going to create a volume which is 200GB in size
  3. 3. Now we can see that our volume has been created
  4. 4. To make use of the volume we require a file system so that we can mount on our system
  5. 5. Once the file system is created we can now mount to folder on our system, mine is mounting to storage
  6. 6. We can now mount this permanently in fstab /etc/fstab

With the below lsblk output, we can see that my ubuntu volume is already using LVM, this is something that can be done during installation. To learn more about create LVM from scratch reference my documentation here.

# file: "lsblk-output"
lsblk
    NAME                      MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS
    sda                         8:0    0 745.2G  0 disk
    ├─sda1                      8:1    0     1M  0 part
    ├─sda2                      8:2    0     2G  0 part /boot
    └─sda3                      8:3    0 743.2G  0 part
    └─ubuntu--vg-ubuntu--lv 252:0    0   100G  0 lvm  /
    sdb                         8:16   0   1.8T  0 disk
    sdc                         8:32   0 931.5G  0 disk

1. Check attributes of the current volume group

sudo vgs
    VG        #PV #LV #SN Attr   VSize    VFree
    ubuntu-vg   1   1   0 wz--n- <743.21g <643.21g

2. We are now going to create a volume which is 200GB in size

sudo lvcreate -L 200G -n storage ubuntu-vg
    Logical volume "storage" created.

3. Now we can see that our volume has been created

# file: "lsblk-after-create"
lsblk
    NAME                      MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS
    sda                         8:0    0 745.2G  0 disk
    ├─sda1                      8:1    0     1M  0 part
    ├─sda2                      8:2    0     2G  0 part /boot
    └─sda3                      8:3    0 743.2G  0 part
    ├─ubuntu--vg-ubuntu--lv 252:0    0   100G  0 lvm  /
    └─ubuntu--vg-storage    252:1    0   200G  0 lvm

4. To make use of the volume we require a file system so that we can mount on our system

sudo mkfs.xfs /dev/ubuntu-vg/storage
    meta-data=/dev/ubuntu-vg/storage isize=512    agcount=4, agsize=13107200 blks
    =                       sectsz=512   attr=2, projid32bit=1
    =                       crc=1        finobt=1, sparse=1, rmapbt=1
    =                       reflink=1    bigtime=1 inobtcount=1 nrext64=0
    data     =                       bsize=4096   blocks=52428800, imaxpct=25
    =                       sunit=0      swidth=0 blks
    naming   =version 2              bsize=4096   ascii-ci=0, ftype=1
    log      =internal log           bsize=4096   blocks=25600, version=2
    =                       sectsz=512   sunit=0 blks, lazy-count=1
    realtime =none                   extsz=4096   blocks=0, rtextents=0

5. Once the file system is created we can now mount to folder on our system, mine is mounting to storage

sudo mount /dev/ubuntu-vg/storage /storage
lsblk -f
    NAME                      FSTYPE      FSVER    LABEL UUID                                   FSAVAIL FSUSE% MOUNTPOINTS
    sda
    ├─sda1
    ├─sda2                    xfs                        591e7b70-727d-44c5-8cb2-cbc452531e60      1.8G     8% /boot
    └─sda3                    LVM2_member LVM2 001       vVWOzE-NGJ8-R2b6-dteC-2Xwo-HQMc-zJQpv4
    ├─ubuntu--vg-ubuntu--lv xfs                        55fa1bbe-29e0-482c-9d59-8bcd6b4c22b6     92.2G     8% /
    └─ubuntu--vg-storage    xfs

6. We can now mount this permanently in fstab /etc/fstab

It’s a general best practice to mount using UUID as it never changes compared to device names.

# file: "/etc/fstab"
# Add entry:
/dev/disk/by-uuid/6838af0e-731b-4431-a72d-08c104781bef /storage xfs defaults 0 0

For UUID of your disk, this can be obtained by running blkid command on your system to print block ids for your devices. Save the entries and run below command and resolve any errors.

sudo mount -a
entry.summary: This post covers a basic walkthrough on steps to take create a logical volume

entry.title: Why DRS/MGN agent failed to install due to “No space left on device” but the volume has sufficient disk space.

link.title: Why DRS/MGN agent failed to install due to “No space left on device” but the volume has sufficient disk space.

entry.content:
1. Problem Statement
  2. Troubleshooting

Problem Statement

During installation you will see standard failure when executing agent installation:

Downloading the AWS Replication Agent onto the source server...

Error: Failed Downloading the AWS Replication Agent
Installation failed.
Learn more about installation issues in our documentation at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/drs/latest/userguide/Troubleshooting-Agent-Issues.html

You can review install logs for context on failure, by default the installation log is saved in the install directory

$ sudo cat aws_replication_agent_installer.log [Truncated]

Error: Failed Downloading the AWS Replication Agent
Installation failed.
Learn more about installation issues in our documentation at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/drs/latest/userguide/Troubleshooting-Agent-Issues.html  [installation_id: 4495e699-4c21-4eee-bac6-c1f6b79ee6db, agent_version: 5.19.0.2023.352.1140, aws_instance_id: i-08907809e46fda714, mac_addresses: 16445040878151, _origin_client_type: installer]
2024-01-13 14:02:22,825 ERROR      Installation was not finished successfully
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "dirrus/installer_shared/installer_main.py", line 451, in download_installer_files
  File "shared/installer_utils/file_utils.py", line 23, in save_files
OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device

Troubleshooting

Based on the error we confirm the available disk space

$ df -hT

Filesystem     Type      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
devtmpfs       devtmpfs  4.0M     0  4.0M   0% /dev
tmpfs          tmpfs     979M     0  979M   0% /dev/shm
tmpfs          tmpfs     392M  5.4M  386M   2% /run
/dev/xvda1     xfs       8.0G  1.6G  6.5G  20% /
tmpfs          tmpfs     300M     0  300M   0% /tmp
/dev/xvda128   vfat       10M  1.3M  8.7M  13% /boot/efi
tmpfs          tmpfs     196M     0  196M   0% /run/user/1000

From the output we can see that our root directory / is 20% utilized with sufficient space to accommodate the agent. However our /tmp directory is mounted on tmpfs and currently does not have sufficient space to accommodate agent based on documentation [1] the DRS agent requires at-least 500MB to install successfully. tmpfs is a temporary filesystem that resides in memory and/or swap partition(s). By default, a tmpfs partition has its maximum size set to half of the available RAM on the system, however it is possible to overrule this value.

Therefore the resolve the issue you can proceed with the following steps:

  1. Temporary override the default value you can proceed with the following command to accommodate the agent 
    mount -o remount,size=1G /tmp
  2. To permanently override this value you can add below entry in /etc/fstab based on desired size 
    tmpfs   /tmp         tmpfs   rw,nodev,nosuid,size=1G          0  0
  3. Depending on whether this is production or testing EC2 instance customer can increase their instance size which in turn allow more memory assignment to tmpfs

After performing method:1 we now see that our tmpfs directory is 1GB

df -hT
Filesystem     Type      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
devtmpfs       devtmpfs  4.0M     0  4.0M   0% /dev
tmpfs          tmpfs     979M     0  979M   0% /dev/shm
tmpfs          tmpfs     392M  5.4M  386M   2% /run
/dev/xvda1     xfs       8.0G  1.6G  6.5G  20% /
tmpfs          tmpfs     1.0G     0  1.0G   0% /tmp
/dev/xvda128   vfat       10M  1.3M  8.7M  13% /boot/efi
tmpfs          tmpfs     196M     0  196M   0% /run/user/1000

We now have a successful agent installation

Source Network already exists for vpc: vpc-0e74d42255ebfc9d9.
The following is the source server ID: s-341d64cdb11d39ef2.
The AWS Replication Agent was successfully installed.

[1] Installation requirements for AWS Replication Agent [2] tmpfs
entry.summary: This article covers a recent issue I encountered whilst assisting customer with troubleshooting “No space left on device” error on AL2023 when sufficient disk space was available.

entry.title: Why is my DRS Drill/Recovery Instance launching in default VPC?

link.title: Why is my DRS Drill/Recovery Instance launching in default VPC?

entry.content:
I have come across a few cases where customers launch recovery instances and need further guidance on understanding the following.

  1. The Problem
  2. Locating the Launch Template
  3. Why This Happens
  4. The Fix

The Problem

  • Why is it launching in default VPC
  • Why is it failing to launch due to no default VPC or subnet specified (VPCIdNotSpecified)

DRS uses EC2 launch templates to launch drill and recovery EC2 instances, these launch templates are created automatically with default settings to launching drill/recovery instances. These are created upon installation of the AWS replication agent.

Locating the Launch Template

Generally the launch templates are available on EC2 dashboard. For ease of use we can access the launch templates directly from DRS console by selecting source –> Launch Settings –> Launch Template.

DRS console path to launch template settings.

By default the subnet is configured to “Don’t include in launch template”.

Default launch template subnet setting.

Why This Happens

The above configuration will be why customer drill/recovery instance launches:

  • In default VPC, if one exists
  • Fail to launch with “An error occurred (VPCIdNotSpecified) when calling the RunInstances operation: No default VPC for this user” should default VPC not exist.

VPCIdNotSpecified error when no default VPC exists.

From the documentation:

Customers that do not have a default VPC must modify the EC2 Launch Template and explicitly define the subnet in which to launch. Failure to do so will result in errors when launching Drill or Recovery instances.

Subnet - Choose the subnet. This is the subnet within which the network interface is located and the Drill or Recovery instance will be launched. Elastic Disaster Recovery selects the default VPC subnet by default (if one exists).

The Fix

To workaround/fix the issue we can modify the Launch Template and specify subnet.

Modifying the launch template to specify a subnet.

These settings can be found under the “Network Settings”.

Network settings section in the launch template.

If a customer does not explicitly set the subnet in the EC2 Launch template this will result in drill/recovery instances launching in default VPC or fail with error if non-exists.
entry.summary: Understanding why DRS drill and recovery instances launch in the default VPC and how to resolve VPCIdNotSpecified errors.

entry.title: Create CloudWatch Alarms for Multiple EC2 Instances & EBS Volumes [Script]

link.title: Create CloudWatch Alarms for Multiple EC2 Instances & EBS Volumes [Script]

entry.content:
I come across a few cases whereby customers are looking for a simplified approach to create cloudwatch alarms for more than 1 resource (e.g I have 30 instances how can I create an alarm for all of them). The below are actually 1 script but can be modified to serve different resources (EBS or EC2) additionally these can serve different metrics below are just examples but you can edit parameters of the put-metric-alarm cli to your use case.

  1. EBS Alarms
  2. Demo
    1. EC2 Alarms

EBS Alarms

The example script below creates a throughput alarm for all volumes in af-south-1 region

#!/bin/bash
listVolumes='/tmp/listvolumes.txt'
volumes=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes --query "Volumes[*].[VolumeId]" --filters Name=tag:Environment,Values=Test --output text --region af-south-1)
echo ${volumes} > ${listVolumes}
for Volumes in `cat ${listVolumes}`
do    
        echo $Volumes    
        aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \    
        --alarm-name ${Volumes} \    
        --alarm-description "Alarm when EBS volume exceeds 100MB throughput" \    
        --metric-name VolumeReadBytes \    
        --namespace AWS/EBS \    
        --statistic Average \    
        --period 300 \    
        --threshold 100000000 \    
        --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \    
        --dimensions Name=VolumeId,"Value=${Volumes}" \    
        --evaluation-periods 3 \    
        --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:af-south-1:[Account ID]:my-alarm-topic
done

This is a helpful note or context the reader should be aware of.

Demo

Before running the script we have no alarms, view our list of volumes

We can also confirm that currently there is no Cloudwatch alarms

Executing the script from terminal, outputs will list all volumes

./VolumeAlarm.sh

vol-085e6db382ac333f8
vol-00d93bdc2097ae508
vol-080d7fe782bb3c5bf
vol-00d406d7778f3bc03
vol-0f9aa70d30ca661c7

Cloudwatch Alarm Dashboard

We can now confirm our alarm by selecting any of the listed volumes

EC2 Alarms

The example script below creates a CPUutilization alarm that alarms when CPU reaches 70% [Can be filtered based on preferences]

#!/bin/bash
listInstances='/tmp/Instances.txt'
instances=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region eu-west-2 --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId]" --filters Name=instance-state-name,Values=running --output text)
echo ${instances} > ${listInstances}
for Instance in `cat ${listInstances}`
do
	 echo $Instance
	 aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
        --region eu-west-2\
        --alarm-name status-check-${Instance} \
        --alarm-description "Alarm when instance experience status check fail" \
        --metric-name "StatusCheckFailed_Instance" \
        --namespace "AWS/EC2" \
        --statistic "Sum" \
        --period 300 \
        --threshold 0 \
        --comparison-operator "GreaterThanThreshold"  \
        --dimensions "Name=InstanceId","Value=${Instance}" \
        --evaluation-periods 1 \
        --alarm-actions "arn:aws:sns:eu-west-2:[Account ID]:StatusCheckFailed" \
        --unit Count
done
entry.summary: This article shares some scripts to simplify the creation cloudwatch alarms for multiple EC2 instances and EBS volumes.

entry.title: Connecting Ansible over AWS Session Manager

link.title: Connecting Ansible over AWS Session Manager

entry.content:
This article aims to explain, how to configure ansible to connect over SSM session manager connection and execute playbooks.

  1. 1. Install aws collections using ansible-galaxy
  2. 2. Enable the plugin in ansible.cfg
  3. 3. Create inventory file
  4. 4. Create and execute a playbook
  5. Troubleshooting
    1. Sample log output from cloudwatch logs:

The purpose of this article is to explain how to execute Ansible playbooks on EC2 instances over SSM Session Manager connection. To achieve this use-case we make use of Ansible Collections, these are a distribution format for Ansible content that can include playbooks, roles, modules, and plugins. For this exercise we will make use of the following plugins:

amazon.aws.aws_ec2 - Get inventory hosts from EC2
community.aws.aws_ssm - This connection plugin allows Ansible to execute tasks on an EC2 instance via the aws ssm CLI

The above plugins form part of the amazon.aws (maintained by the Ansible cloud team) and community.aws (maintained by the Ansible community) collections. These modules and plugins offer a variety of Ansible content to help automate the management of AWS resources.

These are third-party plugins and customers can reach out to vendor and/or community for support issues pertaining these plugins. We can however assist with SSM connection issues and session manager plugin. For everything else, best efforts.

Okay! Let’s put the lab together by preparing our instances.

Controller Instance:
Ansible
SSM Agent
Session manager plugin
python >= 3.6
boto3 >= 1.16.0
awscli (optional)

Target Instance (s) SSM Agent curl installed

Once we’ve prepped our environment, our next steps will be to configure our inventory using the amazon.aws.aws_ec2

1. Install aws collections using ansible-galaxy

ansible-galaxy collection install community.aws

When you install the community.aws collection it will automatically install the amazon.aws collection.

2. Enable the plugin in ansible.cfg

This can be done on global config /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg, for this exercise we will create a local cfg file.

First we create a project folder and cd into that folder:

mkdir project1
cd project1

Generate ansible.cfg file:

ansible-config init --disabled > ansible.cfg

Using your favorite text editor, edit ansible.cfg » find entry: [inventory] and enable EC2 plugin, similar to below:

# file: "ansible.cfg"
[inventory] enable_plugins = amazon.aws.aws_ec2

Once the above is complete, you should now have a ansible.cfg file with plugin enabled for local testing. Next steps will be to create inventory.

3. Create inventory file

To create inventory file, we create aws_ec2.yml in our project directory (Use a YAML configuration file that ends with aws_ec2.{yml yaml}). 
sudo vi aws_ec2.yml

Here we specify our ec2_inventory plugin and set some parameters, can reference docs for parameter options.

# file: "aws_ec2.yml"
##Inventory
plugin: amazon.aws.aws_ec2
regions:
 - "us-east-1"
filters:
 instance-state-name : running
hostnames:
 - instance-id
compose:
  ansible_host: instance-id

Now that we have our inventory file, let’s do a test run by performing a list operation:

{
  "all": {
    "children": [
      "aws_ec2",
      "ungrouped"
    ]
  },
  "aws_ec2": {
    "hosts": [
      "i-04dc05dca9b5ede89",
      "i-0e6ee2fa57c6d1b46"
    ]
  }
}

Inventory test output showing 2 discovered instances.

As we can see from the truncated output, ec2-inventory returned 2 instances (Controller and target) now that we confirmed that this works, lets execute a playbook.

4. Create and execute a playbook

Using your text editor of choice, let’s create a playbook to install nginx. You can reference docs for variable options and for example playbooks for both windows and linux.

# file: "test.yml"
---
- name: Install a Nginx Package on Linux Instance
  hosts: all
  vars:
    ansible_connection: community.aws.aws_ssm
    ansible_aws_ssm_region: us-east-1
    ansible_aws_ssm_bucket_name: testing-inspector-policy
  tasks:
    - name: Install a Nginx Package
      yum:
        name: nginx
        state: present

Using the –limit option will exclude controller from play.

To confirm if the nginx was installed on instance, we can connect via session manager and check if the service is present (inactive):

aws ssm start-session --target i-0e6ee2fa57c6d1b46
Starting session with SessionId: testbench_DRS-024b6e23d7767846e

sh-4.4$ sudo systemctl status nginx
● nginx.service - The nginx HTTP and reverse proxy server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/nginx.service; disabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: inactive (dead)

As expected service is installed but not running due to “present” flag.

Troubleshooting

Before working with customer to address issues set the correct expectations regarding support for third-party tools/applications.

Ansible will establish connection to ec2 instance via Session Manager, we start by verifying if we can connect to the instance using session manager without all the bells and whistles:

aws ssm start-session --target i-023500a698884d434
Starting session with SessionId: lunding_awscli-0ef41eeb1b8cc2296
sh-4.4$

Confirm that session starts without error and run a few commands and confirm that there is no issue with session duration. If you happen to run into any errors you can always review the following documentation for troubleshooting.

If you able to connect to the instance with session manager from terminal, we can rule out SSM. Troubleshooting beyond this point will be on best efforts.

Check for any syntax errors in the following files [ansible.cfg, playbook, inventory, etc]

If the above checkouts, rerun the play using -v, –vv or –vvv depending on the level of verbosity to catch any errors from an ansible perspective.

ansible-playbook -i aws_ec2.yml test.yml -vvv --limit '!i-0b52f059504ba450e'

The verbose output provides detailed information about each step of the playbook execution including connection establishment, module execution, and task results. Review the EXEC commands for an overview of commands executed on the instance.

  • The outputs of the commands can also be reviewed via cloudwatch logs, for more information about saving session data and other session manager preferences you can review this article

Sample log output from cloudwatch logs:

CloudWatch log output showing session activity.

Should you get any errors on logs you can assist with best efforts, alternatively customers can open an issue amazon.aws or community.aws depending on the issue experienced. Once connection is confirmed to be working from a Session Manager perspective all should be good from an SSM perspective, Alternatively customers can use RunCommand to execute ansible playbooks.
entry.summary: How to configure Ansible to connect over AWS Session Manager

entry.title: First steps to building a successful career in I.T

link.title: First steps to building a successful career in I.T

entry.content:
From personal experience, I found that many individuals will opt to study I.T/ICT-related studies with no real context as to what is required to build a successful career within the information technology domain. I have decided to draft this useful guide on how to master the concepts, demonstrate practical skill, get hired, and excel in your chosen field of specialization.

  1. 1. The Road Map
  2. 2. Master the basics
  3. 3. The Practice Lab

1. The Road Map

I know how daunting and/or tempting it is to just dive into the typical I.T course that covers various aspects of the field. I made this mistake in 2013 where I opted for the general course as opposed to linking my career goals to what I needed to learn. Hence the roadmap, I.T is a vast field with various outcomes some courses complement each other for example if I were to be opting for an entry-level post I would look at the combination of the following CompTIA Certs (ITF (Fundamentals), A+ (Technician), N+ (Networking)) these are the basics and should enable you for entry-level positions or in our lingo Tier 1. It’s best to first get your foot in the door and then opt for further studies and/or specialization knowing that you have fundamental certs along with work experience. Further to this, you could look at adding Security+ and/or Linux+ to boost your points with the prospective employer. https://www.comptia.org/home offers 2 paths infrastructure & security, vendor-specific courses can be addressed once you’ve settled in your role.

Link your career goals to what you need to learn. Don’t just dive into a general I.T course.

2. Master the basics

Nowadays anyone can learn anything via the internet which is great however bear in mind that most of these sources barely cover foundational knowledge. No matter your level of expertise a quick and regular revision of the fundamentals will help you break down the most complex of scenarios. Elon Musk beautifully places the latter approach in context by looking at First principles thinking. This link should help with the understanding thereof: https://jamesclear.com/first-principles. I found that the geek’s lesson & other channels like freecodecamp.org are a great way to revise foundational knowledge on various concepts/topics within I.T, I have included the link just in case you feeling a bit tempted to dive right in. https://www.youtube.com/c/GeeksLesson/videos?view=0…

No matter your level of expertise a quick and regular revision of the fundamentals will help you break down the most complex of scenarios.

3. The Practice Lab

Remember certifications might be enough to get you to the interview stage but a mastered and refined skillset will ultimately open employment opportunities. Taking the time and care to build a home lab may help you refine your networking, troubleshooting, administration, and/or OS knowledge skills. This will count in your favor once you in the field, mastering the methodology along with the ability to go HAM practically might make you a real asset for not only your employer but for yourself as well. Many tools might help you with this journey such as virtual machines, online practice labs, or recycling old hardware for your network. It need not be expensive but if it is functioning well you cannot go wrong, Side note my first home lab was built on a virtualized environment using VirtualBox with 1 server and 2 windows clients to practically implement Active Directory and master GPO concepts. Creating snapshots will help in the long run for the break & fix situations.

Certifications get you to the interview, but a mastered skillset opens the door.

This guide from I.T Career questions should help you on your quest https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4O_MxTPmah4
entry.summary: A guide on how to master the concepts, demonstrate practical skill, get hired, and excel in your chosen field of specialization.
